questions.prompt: What should a student place on the calendar before adding study blocks?

options.text: Fixed commitments such as practice, family plans, and rides
Every assignment for the whole month
Rewards for finishing the week
A two-hour study block every night

questions.explanation: The routine starts by mapping the times that are already committed so the plan fits the real week.

questions.prompt: Which choice best describes an anchor block?

options.text: A protected study block for the hardest or highest-priority subject
A long break with no plan
Any class period during the school day
A list of missing assignments

questions.explanation: An anchor block is the first study block placed on purpose so the most important work has a realistic home.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson recommend short review blocks instead of one giant review session?

options.text: Short review blocks are easier to fit into a real week and easier to keep
Short review blocks mean the work is unimportant
Students should never study for more than ten minutes
Review should only happen when there is no homework

questions.explanation: The routine aims for repeatable, realistic blocks that students can actually follow through on.

questions.prompt: A student has soccer on Monday, music lessons on Wednesday, and a family dinner on Friday. Which plan uses the routine well?

options.text: Tuesday math anchor block, Thursday reading review, Saturday vocabulary review
Monday two-hour session right after soccer and nothing else all week
Wednesday study block during music lessons
Friday night giant session after the family dinner

questions.explanation: A strong plan respects fixed commitments and spreads work across realistic open times.

questions.prompt: What should a student check before saying the weekly plan is final?

options.text: Whether there are conflicts, overloaded days, or missing materials
Whether every block is exactly the same length
Whether a friend chose the same schedule
Whether every subject appears every day

questions.explanation: The checking step exists to catch problems before the week starts.

questions.prompt: If all three study blocks are stacked on one already busy evening, what is the best revision?

options.text: Keep the anchor block and move the review blocks to other open times
Make the anchor block even longer so all the work fits
Delete the anchor block and leave only easy work
Leave the overloaded evening unchanged because the plan is already written

questions.explanation: Balanced plans are more realistic and easier to keep than one overloaded night.

questions.prompt: Explain why mapping fixed commitments first makes a weekly study plan stronger.
questions.explanation: Mapping fixed commitments first keeps the plan realistic because study blocks are placed around the time the student truly has available.

questions.prompt: A student misses one review block because of an unexpected appointment. What should they do next?

options.text: Move the short review block to the next realistic open time
Cancel the whole weekly plan
Add the missed work to an already overloaded night
Turn the review block into a giant two-hour makeup session by default

questions.explanation: The routine should be adjusted when the week changes; one missed block does not mean the whole plan failed.

3-electives-study-skills-goal-setting-an.title: Goal Setting and Reflection Checkpoint
3-electives-study-skills-goal-setting-an.description: Baseline quiz for grade 3 Study Skills: Goal Setting and Reflection.

questions.prompt: In grade 3 Goal Setting and Reflection, during guided practice, which response best shows that the student used evidence rather than guessing?
questions.explanation: A strong response names specific evidence and connects it to the answer.

options.text: The student names specific evidence and explains how it supports the answer.
options.feedback: Correct. Evidence plus explanation makes the response stronger.

options.text: The student writes only that the answer looks right.
options.feedback: This choice does not match the lesson evidence.

options.text: The student chooses the longest answer without checking it.
options.feedback: Check the task, labels, and reasoning before choosing.

options.text: The student ignores the task and gives an unrelated fact.
options.feedback: Try again by naming the main concept first.

questions.prompt: A grade 3 task for Goal Setting and Reflection feels confusing during guided practice. What should the student do first?
questions.explanation: The first step is to restate the task and identify the useful information.

options.text: Restate the task and identify the useful information.
options.feedback: Correct. Clarifying the task makes the strategy easier to choose.

options.text: Skip the evidence and write any answer.
options.feedback: This choice does not match the lesson evidence.

options.text: Change the topic to something easier.
options.feedback: Check the task, labels, and reasoning before choosing.

options.text: Copy a sentence without checking whether it fits.
options.feedback: Try again by naming the main concept first.

questions.prompt: Which revision would most improve grade 3 work on Goal Setting and Reflection during guided practice?
questions.explanation: The best revision adds clarity, accuracy, or stronger support.

options.text: Add a specific example, label, quote, data point, or source detail.
options.feedback: Correct. Revision should strengthen the reasoning or support.

options.text: Remove the explanation because the answer is enough.
options.feedback: This choice does not match the lesson evidence.

options.text: Make the response shorter by deleting all evidence.
options.feedback: Check the task, labels, and reasoning before choosing.

options.text: Add a decorative detail that does not support the answer.
options.feedback: Try again by naming the main concept first.

questions.prompt: How does grade 3 Goal Setting and Reflection strengthen transfer during guided practice?
questions.explanation: The module builds transfer by asking students to apply, explain, and revise.

options.text: It asks students to apply a skill, explain reasoning, and revise work.
options.feedback: Correct. Fuller curriculum needs application and transfer, not only exposure.

options.text: It only adds more vocabulary to memorize.
options.feedback: This choice does not match the lesson evidence.

options.text: It replaces all other modules in the subject.
options.feedback: Check the task, labels, and reasoning before choosing.

options.text: It avoids evidence so the task is faster.
options.feedback: Try again by naming the main concept first.
